abstract: Efficient separation of ethylene (C₂H₄) from ternary mixtures containing acetylene (C₂H₂) and ethane (C₂H₆) remains a critical industrial bottleneck in the production of polymer-grade feedstocks. In this work, we report the synthesis and characterization of a robust metal-organic framework, HIAM-111, designed for the preferential adsorption of light hydrocarbons. Adsorption isotherms and thermodynamic studies reveal that HIAM-111 exhibits high selectivity for C₂H₂ and C₂H₆ over C₂H₄, enabling the direct recovery of high-purity ethylene in a single separation step. Specifically, using a Dual-site Langmuir-Freundlich model to evaluate competitive binding, we analyzed the material's performance under industrially relevant conditions. Breakthrough experiments for a ternary C₂H₂/C₂H₄/C₂H₆ (1/90/9) mixture at 298 K demonstrate that HIAM-111 can produce 99.5% pure ethylene with high productivity (0.0248 L/kg). Furthermore, the adsorbent exhibits excellent structural stability across various solvents and maintains consistent performance over multiple cycling tests. These findings, supported by molecular simulations and experimental analysis, highlight HIAM-111 as a promising candidate for efficient, energy-saving olefin purification.
